items.title: Why an MBA/PGDM with Internship is the Future of Management Education in India
items.summary: This article explores how MBA programmes with integrated internships are becoming the preferred path for management education in India, offering practical experience alongside academic learning. The author argues that companies increasingly prefer candidates who have real-world exposure over those with purely theoretical backgrounds. For PMs evaluating talent pipelines or career development programmes, this signals a shift in how entry-level product talent is being trained.

items.title: A Spring Break Commute, Five Dollars, and the State of Multilingual Text-to-Speech
items.summary: A hands-on exploration of the current state of multilingual text-to-speech technology, tested with a $5 budget during a commute. The author evaluates several TTS APIs on naturalness, language coverage, and cost, finding significant variation in quality across languages. A useful reference for PMs evaluating voice and accessibility features for multilingual products.

items.title: Discovery: Where Better Products Take Shape
items.summary: This article argues that product discovery is not a phase but a continuous practice embedded throughout the product development lifecycle. The author outlines a framework for ongoing discovery that integrates user research, hypothesis testing, and cross-functional collaboration. For PMs looking to move beyond periodic discovery sprints to a more systematic approach, this provides actionable guidance.

items.title: Most Businesses Choose the Wrong Marketing Agency (Here's Why)
items.summary: Most businesses select marketing agencies based on portfolio aesthetics or price rather than strategic fit, leading to misaligned expectations and poor results. The author identifies the key questions businesses should ask before hiring an agency, focusing on understanding their target audience and measuring outcomes. PMs involved in go-to-market strategy will recognise the pattern of prioritising output over outcome in vendor selection.

items.title: Why Your Business Can't Afford to Ignore Mobile Anymore
items.summary: Mobile now accounts for the majority of digital interactions across virtually every industry, yet many businesses still treat mobile as a secondary channel. The author argues that mobile-first design and strategy is no longer optional — it is the default expectation for users. PMs building consumer or B2B products should audit their mobile experience as a first-class product surface, not an afterthought.

items.title: Why Feedback Quality Declines as Start-ups Scale
items.summary: As startups grow, the signal-to-noise ratio in customer feedback deteriorates because the user base diversifies and direct access to early customers diminishes. The author identifies structural causes — sales-filtered feedback, support ticket aggregation, and executive insulation — and proposes mechanisms to preserve feedback quality at scale. A critical read for PMs at scaling companies who feel increasingly disconnected from their users.

items.title: Stop Building, Start Validating: A 5-Step Guide for Founders
items.summary: This guide challenges the default founder impulse to build first and validate later, arguing that most failed products suffer from insufficient early validation rather than poor execution. The five-step framework covers problem interviews, assumption mapping, minimum viable experiments, and success metrics definition. For PMs, it reinforces the continuous discovery mindset and provides a practical structure for de-risking new initiatives.

items.title: I Analyzed Top Apps And Found This Common Pattern
items.summary: An analysis of top-performing apps across categories reveals a common structural pattern: a tight activation loop that delivers core value within the first session and creates a natural reason to return. The author maps this pattern across consumer apps from different verticals and extracts principles applicable to any product. PMs designing onboarding and retention flows will find this a useful benchmark against best-in-class products.

items.title: Resend CLI 2.0
items.summary: Resend CLI 2.0 is a major update to the developer tool for the Resend email API, adding local development environments, improved debugging, and team collaboration features. The release reflects growing adoption of Resend as a developer-first email infrastructure layer. PMs building products with transactional email workflows will find the improved local testing capabilities particularly useful for reducing email-related bugs in production.

items.title: Zero-Visibility Finance: Building a Privacy-First Credit Card Insights Tool That Never Asks for…
items.summary: This case study documents the design and build of a financial insights tool that provides credit card analysis without requiring users to share sensitive account credentials. The author explores privacy-preserving architectures and the product tradeoffs involved in building trust with users in a high-sensitivity domain. A valuable read for PMs working on fintech, data products, or any product where user data privacy is a core design constraint.

items.title: Google Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS
items.summary: Google has released Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS, a fast and cost-efficient text-to-speech model with improved naturalness and multilingual support. The release signals Google's continued investment in making high-quality voice synthesis accessible at scale for developers. PMs building voice interfaces, accessibility features, or multilingual products should evaluate this against existing TTS options for quality and cost tradeoffs.

items.title: Why 73% of product managers  expect generalists to define the future
items.summary: A survey of product managers reveals that the majority believe generalist PMs — those with broad skills across strategy, data, design, and engineering — will be more valuable in the AI era than specialists. The findings suggest that as AI handles more execution-layer tasks, the distinctly human skills of synthesis, communication, and judgement become the core PM differentiators. An important data point for PMs thinking about their own career development and skill investment.

items.title: Winning the narrative when markets move fast | GTM Labs
items.summary: In fast-moving markets, the ability to control the narrative is a strategic advantage that goes beyond product quality. This GTM Labs session explores frameworks for crafting compelling positioning that resonates with buyers even when the competitive landscape is shifting rapidly. PMs will find practical guidance on aligning messaging with market momentum rather than chasing it.

items.title: Avoid vanity metrics: evaluating true GTM impact | GTM Labs
items.summary: Many GTM teams track metrics that look good in dashboards but do not reflect real business impact. This session makes the case for replacing vanity metrics with leading indicators that connect directly to revenue and retention. For PMs, the key takeaway is building measurement frameworks that hold GTM teams accountable to outcomes, not activity.

items.title: GTM on a budget: do more with less | GTM Labs
items.summary: Resource-constrained teams can still execute high-impact GTM strategies by focusing on high-leverage channels and ruthless prioritization. The session covers how to identify the minimum viable GTM motion that drives meaningful traction without large budgets. Particularly relevant for PMs at early-stage companies or teams running lean after restructuring.

items.title: Lifecycle metrics that drive retention & advocacy | GTM Labs
items.summary: Retention and advocacy are driven by measurable moments in the user lifecycle, not just by product quality. This session maps the key metrics that signal when users are at risk of churning or primed to become advocates, and how to act on them. PMs will take away a practical framework for instrumenting their product to surface these signals in real time.

items.title: Turning launches into repeatable revenue moments | GTM Labs
items.summary: Most product launches are one-off events rather than repeatable revenue drivers. This session introduces a system for designing launches that generate compounding returns by building on previous momentum. For PMs, the insight is that launch strategy should be engineered into the product lifecycle from the start, not bolted on at the end.

items.title: The AI Pivot: Why Product Management is Evolving into “System Orchestration
items.summary: AI is fundamentally transforming product management, shifting the role from traditional oversight to system orchestration, where PMs must manage complex, AI-driven ecosystems.

items.title: Amazon Buys Globalstar, Delta to Add Leo, The Apple Angle
items.summary: Ben Thompson analyses Amazon's acquisition of Globalstar and its implications for the satellite connectivity market. He explores how satellite-connected devices shift the dynamics of consumer hardware and carrier relationships. A must-read for PMs thinking about connectivity as a platform and the strategic implications of satellite infrastructure becoming mainstream.

items.title: Cybersecurity & Ethical Hacking Explained - Protecting the Digital World.
items.summary: Cybersecurity and ethical hacking guard digital systems by proactively preventing attacks, finding and fixing weaknesses, and safeguarding data worldwide. These practices keep users, businesses, and critical infrastructure safe from evolving threats. For product managers, understanding these concepts helps design more secure offerings and instill confidence in end‑users.

items.title: 🔮 The classified frontier
items.summary: This edition of Exponential View examines the growing role of classified AI research in national security and defence, and how the frontier of AI capabilities is increasingly moving behind closed doors. The piece raises important questions about what this means for the pace of public AI progress and who controls the most powerful models. PMs building on AI infrastructure should understand how geopolitical dynamics are shaping foundation model development.

items.title: We Wired Up Audiences in Cinemas, Gaming Labs, and Concert Halls.
items.summary: A research team used biometric sensors to measure real-time emotional responses from audiences at cinemas, gaming labs, and concerts, generating rich data on engagement and attention. The findings reveal that peak emotional moments do not always align with what creators or product teams expect. For PMs, this is a compelling case for physiological and behavioural research methods beyond traditional surveys and analytics.

items.title: The GetAway Group’s AI Product Engineering Development Plan
items.summary: The article argues that product teams should focus on creating the systems, habits, and decision‑making frameworks that enable rapid, compounding use of AI rather than merely adopting AI tools. By embedding AI into the product development process, organizations can achieve sustainable speed and scale in delivering AI‑powered features.

items.title: The Diderot Effect: Product Management’s Most Expensive Blind Spot
items.summary: The Diderot Effect highlights how adding a single new feature to a product can subtly shift user expectations and perceived value, leading to a continuous cycle of feature requests and potentially diminishing returns. For Product Managers, this means carefully considering the long-term impact of each addition, as it can fundamentally alter user perception and create a need for constant upgrades. Ignoring this effect can result in feature bloat and a less satisfying user experience.

items.title: The Diderot Effect: Product Management’s Most Expensive Blind Spot
items.summary: The article discusses the 'Diderot Effect,' the phenomenon where adding small, incremental features to a product can drastically increase development costs and complexity, often leading to an unsustainable product roadmap. Product Managers need to recognize that seemingly minor additions can have a disproportionately large impact on time, resources, and overall product strategy. Prioritization and strategic roadmap planning are crucial to avoid this expensive blind spot.

items.title: LISA Core
items.summary: LISA Core is an AI memory library that allows users to store, organise, and retrieve context across AI interactions, solving the persistent context problem in current LLM-based tools. It positions itself as a personal knowledge layer that makes AI assistants more useful over time by remembering preferences, projects, and past conversations. PMs exploring AI product design will find this an interesting example of the emerging category of AI memory and personalisation infrastructure.

items.title: Faster learning changes how teams work
items.summary: As AI tools compress learning cycles, teams can validate assumptions and iterate on strategies faster than ever before. This creates a new challenge: organisational structures designed for slower learning loops become bottlenecks. PMs need to redesign team workflows and meeting cadences to match the pace at which insights can now be generated.

items.title: Consent Isn’t a Checkbox: Rethinking User Choice in Digital Products
items.summary: Consent in digital products has been reduced to cookie banners that users dismiss without reading, creating a gap between legal compliance and genuine user autonomy. The author argues for designing consent as a meaningful experience that gives users real control and builds trust over time. PMs should treat consent design as a product quality issue, not just a legal requirement.

items.title: Listen: Not all AI agents are created equal
items.summary: The article emphasizes that AI agents vary significantly in capability, suitability, and performance, so product managers must assess them based on specific use cases rather than treating them as interchangeable. Choosing the right AI agent requires evaluating factors like domain expertise, integration ease, and measurable outcomes to ensure it aligns with product goals.

items.title: MiniAi
items.summary: MiniAi is a compact AI assistant designed for everyday tasks, positioning itself as a lightweight alternative to larger models for quick queries and personal productivity. The product targets users who find full-featured AI assistants overwhelming for routine tasks. An interesting case study in how AI products are being segmented by use case intensity rather than capability.

items.title: Payra: Product Lead - Data Products
items.summary: Payra is hiring a Product Lead for Data Products, a role focused on building data infrastructure and analytics products for their platform. The position requires experience bridging technical data teams and business stakeholders, with a strong emphasis on defining data product strategy. A good opportunity for PMs with a data background looking to own a strategic function at a growing fintech.

items.title: Why Travel Platforms Keep Missing Their Most Valuable User
items.summary: Travel platforms consistently optimise for price-sensitive travelers while overlooking frequent business and premium travellers who generate disproportionate revenue. The author argues that most platforms' features are built for the wrong user segment, leading to churn among their most valuable customers. PMs in marketplace or platform businesses will recognise this pattern of optimising for volume at the expense of value.

items.title: Astra
items.summary: Astra is an AI-powered assistant designed for complex research and multi-step reasoning tasks, positioning itself above general-purpose chatbots for professional use cases. The product targets knowledge workers who need deeper analysis rather than quick answers. Interesting to watch as AI assistants increasingly segment by task complexity and domain expertise.

items.title: Claude Code Routines
items.summary: Claude Code Routines is a new feature from Anthropic that allows developers to create reusable, shareable automation sequences within Claude Code, enabling teams to standardise common workflows like code review, documentation generation, and test writing. This represents a shift from individual AI assistance toward team-level AI workflow management. PMs evaluating AI development tools should note how standardised routines could accelerate onboarding and reduce variance in AI-assisted code quality.

items.title: When AI Becomes Invisible, Trust Becomes Strategy
items.summary: As AI capabilities become embedded invisibly into products, users stop perceiving them as AI features and start expecting them as baseline product behaviour. This shift means that trust becomes the primary competitive differentiator for AI-powered products. PMs should design AI features with an explicit trust strategy, not just a capability roadmap.

items.title: When 'Viable' Stopped Being Enough
items.summary: The article argues that simply launching a minimal viable product (MVP) is no longer sufficient for long-term product success. It highlights a shift towards prioritizing viability – ensuring a product can sustain itself and grow – over rapid, initial releases. Product Managers should focus on building a product that's not just functional but also has a clear path to profitability and user retention, rather than solely chasing speed of delivery.

items.title: Product Work Is Relationship Work - All Things Product Podcast with Teresa Torres & Petra Wille
items.summary: Teresa Torres and Petra Wille explain that product management is fundamentally about building relationships—understanding stakeholders, aligning teams, and continuously learning from users—rather than just generating ideas or specs. Even as AI streamlines idea generation, the core value of a product manager lies in facilitation, negotiation, and empathy to ensure the right problems are solved.

items.title: Not all AI agents are created equal
items.summary: The article introduces a framework that classifies AI agents by their capabilities, integration depth, and business impact, helping product managers prioritize which agent initiatives to pursue first. By aligning agent types with strategic goals and resource constraints, product teams can focus on high‑value use cases and avoid overengineering less effective solutions.

items.title: OpenAI’s Memos, Frontier, Amazon and Anthropic
items.summary: Ben Thompson analyses leaked OpenAI internal memos alongside Amazon's deepening partnership with Anthropic and what it means for the frontier model landscape. He argues that competitive dynamics between AI labs are increasingly shaped by compute access and distribution partnerships rather than model quality alone. Essential context for PMs evaluating which AI providers to build on.

items.title: How to Turn a Boring Corporate Strategy Into a Shared Team Story
items.summary: This article highlights the importance of framing corporate strategy as a shared team story rather than a top-down mandate. By using language that emphasizes milestones and goals the team actively wants to achieve, you can foster greater buy-in and engagement.  Shifting from abstract corporate directives to concrete, team-oriented objectives is key to driving motivation and productivity.

items.title: AI Accelerates Building. It Does Not Accelerate Commitment.
items.summary: The article emphasizes that AI tools can speed up development but not decision-making or commitment.

items.title: AI Agents Don’t Need Your Permission Anymore — What That Means If You’re a Product Manager
items.summary: The article highlights that AI agents now operate without needing user intervention, significantly altering the Product Manager's role.

items.title: I’m Standing Inside Bell Labs’ Legacy. And Thinking About AI.
items.summary: The article describes a visit to Bell Works, a former Bell Labs facility now housing various tech companies. The author reflects on the significance of AI in light of Bell Labs' historical innovation, suggesting a potential resurgence of groundbreaking research and development. This highlights the importance of anticipating future technological shifts and fostering environments conducive to innovation, particularly in areas like AI.

items.title: Cenote
items.summary: Cenote is a data observability and pipeline monitoring tool that helps data teams detect and diagnose issues in their data infrastructure before they impact downstream products and decisions. It positions itself as a layer of reliability on top of existing data stacks. PMs relying on data pipelines for product analytics or ML features will find this category increasingly relevant as data quality becomes a product quality issue.

items.title: 📈 Data to start your week – The jet fuel inflection point
items.summary: This article highlights a potential inflection point in energy markets driven by geopolitical conflict, suggesting a shift in energy supply and demand dynamics. For Product Managers, this signals a need to monitor external factors impacting user behavior and potentially adjust product roadmaps to address changing needs related to energy costs or availability. Understanding these macro trends is crucial for anticipating future user needs and maintaining product relevance.

items.title: Google's Nest Doorbell
items.summary: Google's Nest Doorbell update brings improved AI-powered person and package detection, enhanced night vision, and a redesigned app experience for managing home security. The update reflects ongoing investment in making smart home hardware more reliable and less reliant on cloud processing. For PMs in IoT and smart home products, this release sets a higher baseline for what users expect from AI-powered home security features.

items.title: 🎙️ This week on How I AI: Claude Cowork tutorial for non-engineers + Build your own Slack inbox (for $0)
items.summary: This article promotes a tutorial from How I AI focusing on using Claude, an AI assistant, for collaborative workflows, specifically designed for non-technical users. It also highlights a method for creating a custom Slack inbox for free, offering practical productivity tips.  Product Managers can leverage these resources to explore AI tools for team collaboration and streamline communication processes.

items.title: Claude Cowork 101: How to automate your workday without touching code | JJ Englert (Tenex)
items.summary: This article demonstrates how to leverage Claude Cowork to automate repetitive tasks across various business tools like email and Slack, significantly boosting productivity. It highlights a no-code approach, allowing product managers and teams to integrate AI capabilities into their workflows without requiring technical expertise. The core insight is that AI automation can be readily implemented to streamline daily operations and free up valuable time for strategic work.

items.title: Mythos, Muse, and the Opportunity Cost of Compute
items.summary: The article argues that Aggregation Theory still holds when compute resources are scarce, because firms that can manage and prioritize demand will control the limited supply and gain market power. For product managers, this highlights the strategic advantage of shaping user demand to secure access to constrained compute resources.

items.title: iRobot: Colin Angle. How The Roomba Became a Household Icon
items.summary: This episode of How I Built This tells the story of how Colin Angle turned a bold robotics research project into one of the most recognisable consumer products of the past two decades. The episode explores the product decisions, pivots, and persistence required to build a category-defining business. Essential listening for PMs who want to understand how consumer products achieve mass adoption and cultural relevance.

items.title: How We Built a Voice Agent on Chatzy.ai in Just One Week
items.summary: A hands-on account of shipping a voice agent in one week using AI-assisted development, covering the key architectural decisions and tradeoffs made under time pressure. The team prioritised speed over perfection, using existing APIs and low-code tools to validate the concept before investing in custom infrastructure. A useful case study for PMs evaluating rapid prototyping approaches for voice and conversational AI features.

items.title: Everything is Connected: A Preview of My New Book
items.summary: A preview of an upcoming book arguing that product, design, engineering, and business outcomes are deeply interconnected systems rather than separate concerns. The author challenges the tendency to optimise individual parts in isolation, which often creates local wins but systemic failures. PMs will find value in the systems-thinking framework proposed for navigating cross-functional complexity.

items.title: Most Businesses Don't Need More Posts. They Need This Instead.
items.summary: Most businesses default to producing more content when their marketing underperforms, but the real problem is usually a lack of strategic positioning and clear value proposition. The author argues that clarity of message and audience targeting outperforms volume every time. PMs building growth loops should consider this when evaluating content as an acquisition channel.

items.title: I Built a Fitness Tracker for How You Speak
items.summary: The founder of ExecReps.ai shares how they overcame the 'Gulf of Evaluation' problem — showing users too much data with too little meaning. After building an impressive analytics dashboard, they realised users couldn't answer a simple question: am I improving? The key PM lesson is that users want answers, not data, and simplicity in measurement design drives better outcomes than comprehensiveness.

items.title: X-Pilot
items.summary: X-Pilot is an AI-powered productivity tool designed for power users of X (formerly Twitter), offering advanced scheduling, analytics, and content optimisation features. It positions itself as a professional layer on top of X's native capabilities for creators and marketers. Interesting as a case study in how third-party tools continue to thrive on top of social platforms despite API restrictions.

items.title: Streaks, Nudges, and the Behavioral Science of Showing Up
items.summary: A founder's account of the internal debate around adding streaks to ExecReps.ai, exploring the tension between building a healthy habit product and creating guilt-driven engagement. Drawing on Duolingo's experience and behavioural science research, the team evaluated whether streaks help users build genuine practice or just anxiety. An essential read for PMs working on retention and engagement mechanics.

items.title: Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
items.summary: Keith Rabois shares his contrarian views on building products in the AI era, including his 'barrels vs. ammunition' hiring framework and why he believes talking to customers actively harms consumer product development. He argues AI is fundamentally collapsing the traditional PM role, and that the best builders today work from strong conviction rather than user feedback. A provocative perspective that challenges conventional product wisdom.

items.title: 🔮 Exponential View #569: When the future is uncertain, what do you teach?
items.summary: The article discusses adapting to rapidly changing environments and the importance of leveraging AI tools to stay ahead.

items.title: Community Wisdom: Vibe coding as a team sport, teaching interviewers to surface real signal, and more
items.summary: A curated collection of insights from Lenny's Slack community covering practical topics including using AI-assisted coding collaboratively, improving interview signal quality, and making leadership offsites more effective. Community members share first-hand experiences and frameworks that go beyond theory. Useful for PMs looking for peer-tested approaches to common team challenges.

items.title: 2026.15: Myth and Mythos
items.summary: Ben Thompson explores how dominant tech narratives shape product strategy and competitive dynamics in the AI era, drawing parallels between mythological storytelling and modern platform wars. He argues that companies which control the prevailing narrative gain strategic leverage well beyond their actual technical capabilities. Essential context for PMs navigating AI-driven market shifts and positioning decisions.

items.title: The fall of the house of product:  A heartfelt goodbye
items.summary: A reflective piece marking the end of an era in product-led growth thinking, arguing that the original vision of PLG has been diluted into a marketing buzzword. The author calls for a return to first principles: building products so good that they sell themselves. A thought-provoking read for PMs questioning whether their PLG motion is genuine or performative.

items.title: The financial blind spot that gets product managers fired
items.summary: Many product managers excel at product metrics like NPS and activation rates but are dangerously unaware of the financial metrics boards actually care about — particularly LTV-to-CAC ratio and payback period. The author argues this blind spot is one of the leading reasons experienced PMs get cut in budget reviews. PMs who want to be taken seriously at the executive level need to own their product's financial narrative.

items.title: 💸 Mythos and the mispricing of everything
items.summary: Azeem Azhar argues that financial markets are systematically mispricing AI companies because traditional valuation frameworks fail to account for the compounding, non-linear nature of AI-driven value creation. He draws parallels with previous technological transitions where incumbents were undervalued before markets recalibrated. For PMs at AI-native companies, this has implications for how to communicate product value to investors and boards.

items.title: Anamap
items.summary: Anamap is a market research and competitive intelligence tool that uses AI to map competitive landscapes and identify whitespace opportunities automatically. It targets product and strategy teams who need structured competitive analysis without manually monitoring dozens of sources. A useful addition to the PM toolkit for teams doing regular competitive reviews or market entry assessments.

items.title: Okta: Principal Product Manager, LogIn
items.summary: This job posting highlights the critical role of a Principal Product Manager focused on login experiences, specifically within Okta's Auth0 platform. The position emphasizes ownership of a high-impact area – the 'front door' for millions of users – and requires defining secure, customizable, and performant authentication flows that balance security with a seamless user experience for major brands. It's a technically focused role demanding expertise in building secure-by-design authentication solutions.

items.title: Signifyd: Senior Product Manager, Identity & Intelligence Platform
items.summary: Signifyd is looking for a Senior PM to lead their Identity and Intelligence Platform, focusing on fraud prevention and identity verification at scale. The role sits at the intersection of machine learning, data products, and financial security. A strong opportunity for PMs interested in trust and safety infrastructure.

items.title: Carrum Health: Staff Applied AI Product Engineer
items.summary: Carrum Health is hiring a Staff Applied AI Product Engineer to build AI-powered features for their healthcare navigation platform, with a focus on matching patients to the right providers. The role blends product thinking with hands-on ML engineering, making it unusual in the PM job market. Relevant for technically-oriented PMs exploring hybrid product-engineering roles in health tech.

items.title: Voodoo: Product Specialist Intern - Product
items.summary: Voodoo, one of Europe's leading mobile gaming companies, is offering a Product Specialist internship focused on game product management and player engagement metrics. The role provides exposure to mobile gaming growth loops, A/B testing at scale, and monetisation strategy. A strong entry point for aspiring PMs interested in consumer mobile and gaming.

items.title: Doordash Usa: Staff Product Manager, People Product
items.summary: DoorDash is hiring a Staff PM for their People Product team, focused on building internal tools and experiences that support the company's workforce operations. The role sits at the intersection of HR technology and platform product management, requiring a PM who can navigate complex stakeholder landscapes and build products for internal users at scale. A strong opportunity for PMs interested in enterprise or internal tooling at a high-growth company.

items.title: Commercial vs Internal Products
items.summary: Marty Cagan examines the fundamental differences between building commercial products for paying customers and internal tools for employees, arguing that the constraints, incentives, and success metrics are so different they require distinct PM skill sets. Internal products often lack the forcing function of market competition, which leads to lower quality and slower iteration. A useful framework for PMs transitioning between these contexts.

items.title: An Interview with New York Times CEO Meredith Kopit Levien About Betting on Humans With Expertise
items.summary: The NYT CEO discusses the company's strategy of doubling down on human expertise and trusted journalism as a competitive moat against AI-generated content. She argues that in a world of abundant AI content, scarcity of credible human expertise becomes the premium product. PMs in media or content-adjacent products will find this a valuable case study in differentiation strategy.

items.title: Advice Line with Steve Ells of Chipotle
items.summary: In this Advice Line episode, Guy Raz speaks with Steve Ells, founder of Chipotle, about the strategic decisions that turned a single burrito restaurant into a global fast-casual empire. The episode explores the product decisions, pivots, and persistence required to build a category-defining business. Essential listening for PMs who want to understand how consumer products achieve mass adoption and cultural relevance.

items.title: How quantum computing is transforming  data centers and energy
items.summary: This piece explores how quantum computing is beginning to influence data center architecture and energy efficiency, with early use cases emerging in optimisation problems that classical computers struggle with. For most PMs, quantum computing remains distant from day-to-day product decisions, but understanding the trajectory helps anticipate platform shifts that could reshape cloud infrastructure costs over the next decade.

items.title: Product Roadmaps: How the Best Product Teams Plan for Uncertainty
items.summary: This article highlights the pitfalls of poorly constructed product roadmaps, exemplified by a scenario where prioritization was arbitrary and lacked a clear rationale. It underscores the need for a more robust and strategic approach to roadmap planning, especially when navigating uncertainty. For Product Managers, this emphasizes the importance of data-driven prioritization and stakeholder alignment, moving beyond simple feature lists.

items.title: I built a custom Slack inbox. It was easier than you'd think. | Yash Tekriwal (Clay)
items.summary: Yash Tekriwal from Clay shares how he used AI agents to reduce 150+ daily Slack notifications to ~30 actionable items by building a custom digest and Kanban dashboard. The system categorises messages into action-required, need-to-read, and FYI buckets automatically. A practical example of how PMs can use AI tools to reclaim focus and reduce notification fatigue without waiting for their company to solve it.

items.title: 🔮 Where we’re taking Exponential View next
items.summary: The article outlines the strategic roadmap for Exponential View, focusing on scaling its research studio by selecting an editorial platform that balances depth, flexibility, and audience engagement. The plan emphasizes iterative content experiments, tighter integration with data-driven insights, and expanding collaborative authoring tools to enhance the publication's influence in the tech‑future discourse.

items.title: Anthropic's New Model, The Mythos Wolf, Glasswing and Alignment
items.summary: Ben Thompson analyses Anthropic's latest model release in the context of the broader AI alignment debate and competitive dynamics among frontier AI labs. He examines how safety-focused positioning affects product strategy and market perception for companies like Anthropic. Essential reading for PMs tracking the AI landscape and thinking about how foundation model choices affect their product roadmap.

items.title: The North Star That Wasn't
items.summary: The article critiques the over-reliance on a single 'North Star Metric,' warning that it can lead to narrow optimization and strategic blindness. Product Managers should instead balance a primary goal with complementary counter-metrics to ensure sustainable growth and overall product health.

items.title: FOMO - All Things Product Podcast with Teresa Torres & Petra Wille
items.summary: This podcast episode addresses the overwhelming nature of the rapidly evolving AI landscape for product professionals. It acknowledges the excitement of new AI tools while highlighting the challenge of staying current without experiencing burnout. The conversation likely offers strategies for navigating this constant influx of information and prioritizing impactful AI adoption.

items.title: Flixier Edit by Transcript
items.summary: Flixier has launched a transcript-based video editing feature that allows users to edit video by editing text, automatically cutting or rearranging footage to match the modified transcript. The feature dramatically lowers the barrier to video editing for non-technical creators. For PMs in content or media products, this is a compelling example of how AI can unlock new user segments by abstracting away technical complexity.

items.title: Let's Read Continuous Discovery Habits Together (April 2026)
items.summary: This article announces a community read-along of the book 'Continuous Discovery Habits', offering monthly reading guides with key concepts and video resources. For Product Managers, this signals a valuable opportunity to deepen their understanding of ongoing user research and iterative product development practices. Participating can provide structured learning and community support for implementing these habits.

items.title: Building Banani: How a Canvas-First AI Designer Is Raising the Floor on Product Design
items.summary: Banani is an AI design platform democratizing UX design by providing a simple, canvas-first interface accessible to anyone. This is particularly valuable for resource-constrained teams and early-stage startups who often lack dedicated designers, allowing them to rapidly iterate on designs and improve user experiences without requiring extensive design expertise.

items.title: How will OpenAI compete?
items.summary: Benedict Evans argues that OpenAI faces four fundamental strategic challenges: no clear competitive moat, a rapidly shifting product landscape, the need to cross the chasm without existing distribution, and the difficulty of building consumer products on top of commodity models. He questions whether OpenAI's massive user base translates into durable strategic advantage. A clear-eyed analysis of the competitive dynamics shaping the AI product landscape.

items.title: Product Coaching and AI
items.summary: Marty Cagan explores how AI is changing the nature of product coaching and mentorship, arguing that while AI can accelerate skill development in some areas, the fundamentally human aspects of product leadership — judgement, empathy, and stakeholder navigation — remain beyond AI's reach. He suggests coaches should focus on these irreplaceable skills rather than competing with AI on technical knowledge. A thoughtful perspective for PMs investing in their own development.
